summary: Comprehensive product-ideal blueprint for a workspace-native conversation system. Establishes a clear canonical-vs-provider ownership boundary maintained consistently across all lifecycle flows. Entity taxonomy, when/then chains, and means blocks are self-contained and recoverable. The enforces block carries significant accumulated behavioral knowledge but mixes architectural invariants with UI-level edge-case guardrails, slightly diluting density and abstraction discipline.
strengths: Thorough entity taxonomy with explicit ownership semantics (canonical vs provider-owned) sustained throughout the file
Rich causal chains cover session lifecycle, provider switching, compaction triggers, import normalization, and idle-watchdog arming with minimal interpretive slack
Locally self-describing: a reader with only the language root can recover the full system intent, entity relationships, and behavioral contracts without external references
Accumulated enforces rules encode real operational edge-case knowledge (queued message batching, process-selector blocking, watchdog re-arming after restart, websocket reconnect grace periods)
Provider-polymorphism section cleanly specifies per-provider binding contracts while keeping the canonical session model stable across all providers
weaknesses: The enforces block conflates foundational architectural invariants with incremental UI-level behavioral fixes (e.g., border-attached floating treatments, keystroke re-render suppression) without structural grouping, making it harder to distinguish load-bearing rules from refinements
Some enforces entries read as accumulated bug-fix specifications rather than product ideals, pushing the abstraction level toward implementation guidance in places
The file is long enough that the single flat enforces block would benefit from thematic subsections (ownership, lifecycle, watchdog, presentation) to aid scanning and maintenance
